data.title: Karman: Shooting Higher After Its IPO
data.description:
Shares of Karman Holdings (NYSE:KRMN) have skyrocketed higher after its initial public offering. Following a strong pricing process, shares rose by nearly 50% after its public offering, as investors like the positioning of this missile, defense & space business.

While the business is very profitable, and is showing impressive growth, the issue is that of valuations, as these have run quite high to about 13 times sales and a very elevated earnings multiple. Given these valuations, this remains a show-me-first story, certainly after the optimism priced in soon after the public offering here.

That said, the interesting positioning warrants continued interest in the firm, as I look forward to the upcoming results and 2025 outlook in the upcoming earnings release, but for now, I am not going along the ride here.

Missile, Defense & Space

Karman focuses on the design, testing, manufacturing and sale of critical systems for existing and emerging missile, defense and space programs. The company in particular derives revenues from integrated payload protection, propulsion and interstage system solutions, applied in a wide range of current and new programs.

The company claims that it derives revenues from no less than 100 programs within space, missile, hypersonic and defense applications, from about 70 different customers. The company nonetheless relies on a small customer group for about two-thirds of sales, including many governmental clients.

The company is split pretty evenly between three market types: including missiles and IDS, hypersonic and SMD and Space & Launch. Propulsion Systems, as a product family, makes up nearly half of sales. This is complemented by payload protection (responsible for about a third of sales), with the remainder of revenues generated from aerodynamic interstage.

In these markets, the company is working with and competing against firms like Lockheed Martin (LMT), SpaceX, Boeing (BA) and Northrop Grumman (NOC), to name a few.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

Karman aimed to sell 21.1 million shares at a preliminary price between $18 and $20 per share, with final pricing set at $22 per share. With 8.4 million shares sold by the company itself, the company raised gross proceeds of $185 million.

With a total share count of approximately 132 million shares, the company was awarded a $2.9 billion equity valuation at the offer price. This valuation excludes a $428 million net debt load as of the third quarter of 2024, as net debt is seen closer to $250 million if we factor in net cash proceeds from the public offering. This grants the company a $3.15 billion enterprise valuation at the offer price.

The company has seen spectacular growth in recent years. In the year 2022, the company generated $226 million in sales, on which it reported operating profits of $20 million. Revenues rose by nearly 24% to more than $280 million in 2023 as operating profits more than doubled to $48 million.

The pace of growth was maintained in the first three quarters of 2024. Revenues in the first nine months of 2024 rose by 24% to $254 million, on which solid operating profits were reported at $49 million. Growth is set to be maintained in the fourth quarter, with full year sales seen up 24% to $346 million, as near 20% operating profit margins yield operating profit potential around $70 million.

If we factor in a 5% cost of debt on pro forma net debt and assume a statutory tax rate, I see net earnings potential at around $43 million. This would be equal to earnings of around $0.32 per share, indicating that expectations are high. Expectations have even more risen substantially as shares have risen to the $32 mark soon after the public offering, pushing up the enterprise valuation to around $4.5 billion.

Based on the revenue discussions, shares traded around 13 times sales and about a hundred times earnings, all rather demanding valuations. That however does not factor in about $16 million in annual amortization charges, as that could add about ten cents to earnings per share, but even in such a case, expectations remain very high.

Concluding Thoughts

The biggest risk in this story seems to be the valuation, as shares trade at rather demanding multiples here. Other risks include the reliance on a few large customers, as the current DOGE movement acts as a real potential threat to the business as well.

Other risks include that of technological developments, leaving the solutions provided by the business as ineffective, regulation on the industry, technological failures, as well as cybersecurity threats, as well as reliance on the US Defense budgets. That said, a funded backlog of $550 million as of September 2024 looks rather healthy, at over 1.5 times annual sales.

Nonetheless, the valuation argument is the largest risk in my view. All this makes me very cautious at current levels, as I am taking a wait-and-see approach to learn more about the business and its growth targets for 2025, before re-considering this neutral but cautionary stance.

data.title: MSD: Protected Against Trade Tensions, But Very Expensive
data.description:
The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund (NYSE:MSD) is a closed-end fund that investors can use as a way of getting a high level of current income along with exposure to the debt markets of various emerging market nations. There are relatively few of these funds around, although emerging market debt funds are somewhat more common than emerging market equity funds. This could be partially due to the fact that investing in emerging markets lost some of its appeal following the 1997 Asian financial crisis and the 2008 global financial crisis. After all, following the latter of these events, the United States began to deliver outsized returns as investment capital began to flow into this nation as something of a safe haven trade.

However, it is worth noting that emerging market bonds (EMB) have outperformed domestic investment-grade bonds (AGG) on a total return basis since January 2008:

We can see though that emerging market bonds still did worse than domestic junk bonds over the entire period. The majority of the underperformance came after the COVID-19 pandemic though, as we can see that emerging market bonds were actually beating domestic junk bonds on a total return basis until late 2021. This is at least partially due to the fact that emerging market bonds tend to have higher yields than many domestic investment-grade ones. We can see this by looking at the yields of the indices tracking emerging market bonds and domestic bonds:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index

5.14%

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index

3.74%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.55%

When it comes to bonds, higher-yielding assets are generally preferable to lower-yielding bonds for investors seeking to earn a high level of total return. This is especially true if you own enough bonds to diversify away the risk of defaults. Most closed-end funds and indices do this, so generally speaking, the higher-yielding assets will provide a better total return over the long term. This is exactly what we see in the chart above.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is able to provide an even higher yield than any of the above indices. As of the time of writing, this fund boasts a very impressive 13.27% yield, which compares very well to just about anything in the market. As we can see in this chart, the fund compares very well to its peers:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

13.27%

Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund (TEI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

10.52%

Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund (EDF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

14.17%

Western Asset Emerging Markets Debt Fund (EMD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

10.52%

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt (EDD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

13.47%

As regular readers likely know, I do not ordinarily like to include two funds from the same fund house (in this case Morgan Stanley) when assembling a peer comparison. However, as already mentioned, there are only relatively few closed-end funds in existence that invest in the emerging markets debt sector, so the inclusion of both Morgan Stanley funds was necessary in order to have at least five funds in the chart.

As we can see, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund represents the median yield out of the peer group. The fund has a higher yield than two of its peers, including the popular Western Asset Emerging Markets Debt Fund, but it cannot compete with the very high-yielding Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund. This may discourage some investors from investing in this fund due to the fact that a higher level of income can be obtained by favoring some of its peers. However, as I pointed out in a recent article:

The market tends to value closed-end funds based on their yields. As such, when we see a fund with a yield that is substantially above that of its peers, it can be a sign that the market doubts the fund’s ability to sustain its payout.

This works in reverse as well, as a fund with a low yield when compared to its peers is generally viewed as having a sustainable distribution relative to the market.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund obviously does not have an outsized yield relative to its peers. This suggests that the market does not harbor any doubts about the fund’s ability to sustain its distribution. That is something that should prove appealing to anyone who is seeking a safe and secure source of income. Of course, we should still have a look at the fund’s portfolio and finances in order to make our own judgment call about whether or not that is the case. Naturally, this will be one of the tasks that we focus on over the remainder of this article.

Performance Analysis

As regular readers may remember, we previously discussed the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund late in the summer of 2024. My previous article on this fund was published to Seeking Alpha on September 5, 2024, so approximately five months have passed since that time. A lot has happened in five months, but one of the major events that impacted the performance of emerging market securities has been the election of Donald Trump to his second term as U.S. president. Mr. Trump’s economic policies are rather protectionist in nature as one of his agendas is to increase manufacturing activity in the United States and provide companies with incentives to move their facilities back to the United States if they want to sell to U.S. consumers. This is very important because many emerging markets are hubs of manufacturing activity and export their products to the rest of the world. For example, China, Russia, Vietnam, and Brazil all have very large trade surpluses:

Most people would call those five emerging markets, and some people might include Saudi Arabia and Taiwan among the emerging market nations. The United States, on the other hand, traditionally runs a trade deficit with several of the above nations, including the emerging market ones. President Trump’s agenda thus could impact the economies of many emerging market nations, and if the balance of trade becomes more even, it will reduce the ability of exporting nations to obtain U.S. dollars. As we will see in this article, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund holds a lot of emerging market debt that is denominated in U.S. dollars, so if the ability of these countries to obtain U.S. dollars is reduced, it could put pressure on their ability to service their debts. Thus, an increasingly protectionist trade stance on the part of the United States increases the risk of investing in some of the securities that are held by this fund and the overall risks of the fund itself.

As I explained in a recent article, an improvement in the trade deficit that the United States runs with the rest of the world should strengthen the U.S. dollar against foreign currencies. This would actually cause foreign currency-denominated bonds to decrease when measured in terms of U.S. dollars. The effect here would likely be greater for emerging markets than for developed markets due to emerging markets being more dependent on exports to the United States as a source of economic strength and growth. However, there would not necessarily be an impact on the U.S. dollar-denominated debt of emerging markets (other than a possible price decrease due to rising risks as discussed in the previous paragraphs). The fund’s balance between U.S. dollar-denominated debt and foreign currency-denominated debt will thus have an impact on its performance as well.

The U.S. dollar started rising against many foreign currencies prior to the U.S. national election date. One reason for this is that the market began to suspect that Mr. Trump would win the election back in October and began to price the U.S. higher as an expected result of the policies that he was campaigning on. As we can see here, the U.S. dollar index (DXY) has risen by 5.49% since October 1, 2024:

As such, the fund would have started facing headwinds in early October, with the magnitude of these headwinds dependent on the amount of foreign debt that the fund holds.

Despite these headwinds, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund has held up pretty well since our last discussion. In fact, shares of the fund are up 3.90% since that article was published:

As we can see from the chart, this is significantly better than the 1.24% decline in the U.S. dollar-denominated emerging market bond index over the same period. This is admittedly somewhat confusing, given that this fund will be holding at least some of the same assets as the index. However, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is actively managed, which can give it an advantage over an index fund in certain market conditions. After all, this fund’s management can generally pick and choose what bonds the fund purchases, as opposed to simply being beholden to purchasing whatever is in the index. That could account for some of the strong performance. Another potential factor could be the fund’s share price outperforming its underlying portfolio for whatever reason, which reduces the fund’s overall value proposition. At any rate, the substantial outperformance relative to the index suggests that we should have a closer look at the fund’s portfolio to see what exactly could be the cause of this outperformance. After all, we do not want to just jump in and buy an overpriced asset.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund did not outperform the index solely on a share price basis. As I pointed out in the previous article on this fund:

A simple look at a closed-end fund’s share price performance does not necessarily provide an accurate picture of how investors in the fund did during a given period. This is because these funds tend to pay out all of their net investment profits to the shareholders, rather than relying on the capital appreciation of their share price to provide a return. This is the reason why the yields of these funds tend to be much higher than the yield of index funds or most other market assets.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund paid out distributions to its investors over the five-month period. These distributions provided an additional return that is not accounted for in a simple chart of the fund’s share price performance. We should include them to see how investors in this fund really did, and when we do that we get this alternative chart:

As we can see, shareholders in the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund received a 10.53% total return over the approximately five-month period. This is much better than the 1.31% total return that investors in the index fund received, although including the distributions generally resulted in a better showing for both assets. The high total return provided by the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund seems likely to attract investors overall, and it is something that most of us will appreciate. After all, we all like to beat the market.

However, it is important to note that past performance is no guarantee of future results. As such, let us spend the remainder of this article looking at this fund in more depth and attempting to determine how it is likely to perform going forward.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund has the primary investment objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income. This objective makes a great deal of sense when we consider the strategy that this fund employs, which is described in its most recent annual report:

The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objectives by investing, under normal circumstances, at least 80% of its managed assets in emerging markets domestic debt. To the extent that that Fund invests in derivative instruments that the Adviser believes have economic characteristics similar to debt securities of government and government-related issuers located in emerging market countries and of entities organized to restructure outstanding debts of such issuers, such investments will be counted for purposes of meeting the Fund’s investment objective.

An emerging country is any country that the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development or similar major financial institution has determined to have a low or middle economy or countries included in the Index.

The Adviser invests the Fund’s assets in emerging country debt securities that provide a high level of current income, while at the same time holding the potential for capital appreciation if the perceived creditworthiness of the issuer improves due to improving economic, financial, political, social or other conditions in the country in which the issuer is located. Currently, investing in many emerging country securities is not feasible or may involve unacceptable political risks.

As opportunities to invest in debt securities in other countries develop, the Fund expects to expand and further diversify the emerging countries in which it invests. While the Fund generally is not restricted in the portion of its assets which may be invested in a single country or region, it is intended that, under normal conditions, the Fund’s assets will be invested in issuers in at least three countries.

This description makes it very clear that the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund invests in debt securities issued by countries in emerging markets. This is exactly what we would expect from the fund’s name, as well as its fixed-income classification. As I stated in a previous article:

Bonds by their very nature are income securities, as they do not deliver any net capital gains over their lifetimes. This makes sense, as an investor will purchase a bond at face value and receive face value back when the bond matures. The only investment return for a bond held over its entire lifetime is the coupon payments made to the bond’s owner. Thus, bonds do not deliver capital appreciation over their lifetimes.

As a result of this, any bond fund should have a current income objective. After all, most bonds are not capable of delivering any net capital gains. There is one exception to this, though, and that is that foreign currency-denominated bonds might be able to deliver net capital gains when measured in U.S. dollars. This happens when the U.S. dollar declines against whatever currency the bond is denominated in because the face value will, when the bond matures, convert into more U.S. dollars than the investor needed to use to obtain the bond in the first place. The same thing is true for any bond that is denominated in a currency other than the investor’s own currency. This does not mean that the bond actually appreciates in value, though, as the bond itself does not deliver a net gain when measured in terms of its own currency denomination. It still might be important to the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund though, since this fund evaluates its performance in U.S. dollar terms.

As the above description states, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund invests primarily in debt securities. The fund’s third-quarter 2024 holdings report provides the following asset allocation as of September 30, 2024:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Fixed-Income Securities

96.4%

Warrants

0.8%

Money Market Funds

4.3%

U.S. Treasury Bills

0.2%

As we can see, the overwhelming majority of the fund’s assets are invested in fixed-income securities, which is pretty much what we expected to see. Curiously, though, the fund’s allocation to fixed-income securities is down somewhat from the figures that were provided in its semi-annual report, which we saw in the previous article. Please recall that in the previous article, we saw this asset allocation chart for June 30, 2024:

Thus, we see the following changes over the June 30, 2024, through September 30, 2024, period:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets as of June 30, 2024

% of Net Assets as of September 30, 2024

% Change

Fixed-Income Securities

98.1%

96.4%

-1.7%

+0.8%

Money Market Fund

+2.9%

U.S. Treasury Bills

No Change

The decrease in the fund’s fixed-income securities allocation in exchange for an increase in its money market holdings is rather interesting. This could suggest a risk-off trade, which, unfortunately, appears to have cost the fund a certain amount of return. As we can see here, the emerging markets bond index increased by 6.64% over the June 30, 2024 through September 30, 2024 period:

We can see that emerging markets bonds not only delivered a very positive return over the period, but they actually managed to outperform the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the period. It is, to put it mildly, very rare for any bond index to outperform common stocks.

As some readers might remember, though, the domestic bond market was also fairly strong over the period in question. During the months of July, August, and the first half of September 2024, investors were very aggressively purchasing domestic bonds in anticipation of the Federal Reserve’s projected September federal funds reduction. However, domestic bonds did not manage to outperform the S&P 500 Index as investment-grade bonds were only up 5.22% and junk bonds were only up 4.42% over the same period:

Thus, the fund appears to have cost itself some potential returns by selling off some of its emerging market bonds in exchange for cash over the period. We do not, however, know exactly when the fund made this trade, so we cannot determine the exact magnitude of the sacrificed gains. The closer that the fund made this trade to mid-September (the emerging markets bond index peaked on September 19), the more returns the fund gave up.

Earlier in this article, I stated that a protectionist trade policy on behalf of the United States might make it more difficult for emerging market nations to obtain the U.S. dollars that they require to service their debt. The degree to which this is a problem depends heavily on the country’s own trade surplus with the United States. The chart may be slightly confusing, as a negative value in the balance of trade column indicates that the country has a positive balance of trade with the United States. In other words, the United States has a trade deficit with the country in question. It would, at least in theory, be those countries that would be most at risk should protectionist trade policies make it more difficult for them to obtain the U.S. dollars that are needed to service their international debts. As we can see though, the fund appears to have done a pretty good job diversifying its country risk. The only three countries that account for more than 3% of the fund’s net assets and have trade surpluses with the United States are Ecuador, Ghana, and Mexico. None of these countries has more than a 5% weighting in the fund, so none of them account for a particularly large proportion of the fund’s assets.

The exact measures that the new U.S. administration will take remain a point of uncertainty. Indeed, Rabobank pointed out yesterday that the Administration appears to be willing to negotiate with other countries in respect to trade policies. As such, it remains difficult to determine our exact risks here, but the fund’s lack of significant exposure to any individual country is comforting.

Valuation

Shares of the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund are currently trading at a 2.57% premium on net asset value. This is a slightly better price than the 3.89% premium that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month, but it does still represent a premium to the value of the underlying portfolio. As such, anyone purchasing shares of the fund today is paying more for the underlying assets than they are actually worth.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is currently looking very expensive historically. Over the past three years, the fund has traded at prices ranging from a 16.12% discount to a 4.56% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 9.26% discount on net asset value. That is a substantially better price than the current one, which suggests that this fund is very expensive compared to its long-term average.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is also trading at a very high price compared to its peers. We can see that in this comparison chart:

As we can see, three of the five funds are trading at fairly large discounts on net asset value with the median price being a 7.16% discount. As such, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is quite expensive when compared to its peers. Admittedly, this is perhaps the best-run fund out of this group, but its current price certainly looks far too expensive.

The best move here is probably to wait until this fund begins trading at a discount before purchasing its shares.

Expenses

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund has an expense ratio of 1.26%, which will likely seem high to any investor who is used to the sub-1% ratios of most index funds. However, this fund is not especially expensive when compared to its peers:

The fact that the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund does not employ leverage (or at least it is not currently employing leverage) gives it an advantage over its peers in the expense department. That makes a lot of sense since the lack of leverage means that this fund does not have to pay carrying costs for the leverage. However, the fund also has a very low expense ratio even if we ignore the leverage-carrying costs. Thus, for the most part, investors who dislike high expenses will likely be reasonably satisfied here.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is one of the better emerging markets closed-end funds around. This fund boasts a substantially higher yield than most domestic bond funds or indices, and it also beats the emerging market debt index as a yield play. The fund also boasts a portfolio that is very well-diversified by country, which could prove to be a good thing to have as the United States government uses various protectionist policies to try and improve its balance of trade.

The big problem here is the fund’s price, as the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is currently trading at a premium and is at a price that is far above its long-term average. It is certainly worth considering if it swings to a discount, however.

data.title: 2 'Must-Buy' Growth Stocks In Today's Expensive Market
data.description:
No matter from what angle we look at the US stock market, we see valuations are historically high.

Goldman Sachs has recently done an analysis, excluding the US tech firms, and still finding that the US market is priced at 19.3x P/E, far above the historical P/E of 15x. That's a 20-year peak.

The S&P 500, in particular, is a very different index today compared to the 1970-80s, when the largest firms were capital-intensive manufacturing companies. Today, most of the tech firms, or Mag7, which make up around 50% of the index, are asset-light operations with record-breaking profit margins, which certainly justifies higher valuation, but the question is, how much higher?

If we include tech firms, the P/E of the total market comes to 22.2x. For the S&P 500, on a trailing twelve-month basis, it's as much as 30.5x. Nasdaq is currently priced at 42.6x.

Yes, US GDP growth is far superior to the rest of the world, particularly to Europe, but US stocks now make up around 70% of the MSCI World Index, the highest since the 70s.

The combination of a strong USD, high valuations, low cash allocation of money managers and high investor expectations leaves little room for error and that's why making sure we own the right basket of equities in our portfolio becomes more important than ever.

Now, high P/E ratios don't necessarily spell trouble for the US stock market, and I certainly don't think we are in a bubble, but personally, I am currently sitting on my largest-ever cash pile, being very selective about my asset allocation.

For instance, during previous bubbles, such as the Nifty-Fifty in the 60s we saw P/Es as high as 90x and the DotCom bubble sent the P/E to as high as 50x for the S&P 500, we are still far away from these levels.

However, eventually, the market valuation will revert to its mean, and simply buying the market as such won't do the trick to earn high enough ROR to justify the risks. Simply put, the risk/reward is not favorable for the time being.

Right now, the expectation for the S&P 500 is to deliver only a meager 5% annual ROR over the next three years.

Having said that, today I want to show you where I still see good opportunities and where I was recently increasing my exposure.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL)

Google has recently reported its Q4 earnings, and despite the solid results, the stock was punished, sending the stock roughly 10% lower since the report.

The profit came in better than expected, but Google missed analysts' expectations on revenue. However, it wasn't either the profit or revenue that sent the stock lower. Instead, the slower-than-expected Google Cloud growth and the humongous $75B CAPEX budget for 2025, up 43% from 2024, spooked investors.

From my perspective, this drop in stock price, even though not massive, is a perfect opportunity for investors to buy "quality at a discount." Google's fundamentals have been continuously improving over the last two years, with the business delivering strong double-digit growth while catching up in the AI race.

For Q4 2024, Google reported $96.5B in revenue, up 12% YoY, missing analyst's expectations by $170M, amidst very high expectations. For the full year of 2024, Google reported 14% growth, which shows that the top line is likely decelerating, but I expect deceleration to be the main story of 2025 among most of the Mag7 businesses after two years of strong growth.

With Google's $350B business run rate, the slowdown is inevitable.

But that shouldn't scare you away, instead Google has reengineered its cost basis and the company has expanded its operating margins from 27% a year ago to 32% and I expect there is more to come, similarly as we see with Meta Platforms (META).

Now, I don't want to go in-depth into the Q4 2024 earnings that I already covered in my previous article, I just want to highlight Google's Cloud performance.

For the quarter, analysts were looking for a 32% growth, yet Google Cloud grew "only" 30%, slowed down by infrastructure constraints.

To put it into perspective, compared to its largest peers Google still showcased one of the strongest growth rates, but its market share is also the smallest:

  • Google Cloud: 30% Growth, Market share of 11%
  • Microsoft Azure (MSFT): 31% Growth, Market share of 20%
  • Amazon AWS (AMZN): 19% Growth, Market share of 31%

Google's Cloud growth is very important to the whole company, as its seen as one of the next key pillars to drive its bottom line over the next years.

During the earning calls, the management also confirmed that AI-powered cloud offerings are attracting new customers, such as Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF) and MercadoLibre (MELI), and that the number of first-time contracts doubled in 2024 compared to 2023.

However, this also brings me to the point of the $75B announced CAPEX for 2025, which will also be spent on AI infrastructure. That's an increase from $52.5B spent in 2024 and the fair question to ask is how much does Google need to spend, in order to unlock the growth.

2025 will likely be a year of growth deceleration and FX headwinds as 51% of Google's revenue is generated outside of the US as the USD remains strong, so I would like to see Google be more conscious of its spending.

Following the stock price drop, Google is currently valued at 22.8x its Blended P/E.

Over the past 15 years Google managed to grow its EPS at a rate of 18% annually. The growth moving forward, according to FactSet, is expected to be slightly slower:

  • 2025: EPS of $8.91E, YoY growth of 11%
  • 2026: EPS of $10.16E, YoY growth of 14%
  • 2027: EPS of $11.73E, YoY growth of 15%

However, the combination of low 20x P/E valuation and 11-15% annual EPS growth for a quality business as Google is, is something that is too attractive to pass on, particularly as the S&P 500 is expected to deliver only 5% annualized ROR.

I think, once the DOJ antitrust case is cleared, with softer remedies than breaking up Google or forcing a divestiture of Google Chrome, Google can once again trade around its 15Y P/E of 24.4x

As it stands right now, Google presents one of the best risk/reward profiles among the Mag7, and investors can expect up to 16.5% annualized ROR, with my price target of $270-$300/share by the end of 2027.

ASML Holding N.V. (ASML)

Following a transitory 2024, ASML is back to reporting strong earnings and is likely to see the recovery of its core, non-AI business in H2 2025.

The Q4 2024 earnings were a record quarter for the company, with net sales of €9.3B, above ASML's previous guidance for the quarter. The results in the quarter were driven by the two high NA systems recognized during the quarter, as well as by ever-increasing Installed Base revenue.

Here, I want to make the case for ASML's reoccurring revenue, particularly as many investors avoid investing in the cyclical semiconductor industry. As ASML's existing user base and equipment base increase, the company is collecting revenue through maintenance, support services, and equipment upgrades, providing stability and growth even during periods of weaker sales.

Fellow investors often ask me what I think about the tariffs the US has recently started rolling out and its impact on ASML's business. While the semiconductor business is very sensitive to global trade dynamics, particularly as ASML's supply chain spans many countries, I actually see the tariffs as value-accretive to ASML, as more chips will likely be produced domestically in the US, and that will require new DUV and EUV equipment for the fabs in the US, benefiting ASML.

At the same time, if the global trade war escalates, the Netherlands, the country where ASML is based, may be less likely to cooperate with US-export restrictions, potentially, in the future, selling even the more advanced EUV chip-making devices to China.

Hand in hand, ASML's position is unchallenged by any other competitor, particularly as Intel's (INTC), Samsung's (OTCPK:SSNLF), and Taiwan Semiconductor's (TSM) fabs were redesigned to accommodate ASML's cutting-edge EUV technology, with switching costs far outweighing the benefit of switching to any new, unproven technology.

In terms of forward guidance, the management expects 2025 revenue to land between €30-€35B with a continuously strong gross margin of 51-53%.

At the midpoint of €32.5B, this would mean a year-over-year growth of 15% compared to the full year 2024.

ASML also sticks to its 2030 guidance of €44-€60B, but this view, in my opinion, is relatively cautious and does not fully consider the tailwind of increased AI chip demand. Even though 2030 is still five years away, I would expect ASML's revenue to land at the upper end of its long-term guidance.

For those seeking dividends, ASML was historically seen as a dividend growth contender, however, due to business challenges of the last two years, the dividend growth plateaued.

As the business recovers in H2 2025 and beyond, I expect ASML will return to its generous dividend growth policy, perhaps with a dividend growth of around 10-12% annually.

What's worse, and what I criticized previously, is the inefficient buyback program. In Q4, when ASML's shares were under pressure, the management failed to allocate any capital to buybacks and did not take the opportunity to reduce the float when shares were trading at around €600/share.

Instead, the business is now repurchasing shares, when the share price already crossed €700/share.

ASML's valuation is currently at a P/E of 31.3x its forward 2025 EPS.

This is currently below the normalized P/E of 33.15x, implying a slight discount.

However, more important is the forward growth FactSet is expecting (for ADR shares):

  • FY2025: EPS of $24.74E, 24% YoY growth
  • FY2026: EPS of $30.21E, 22% YoY growth
  • FY2027: EPS of $36.53E, 21% YoY growth

The forward growth expectations are also well above its 15Y annual EPS growth of 16.8%.

This brings me to the point that if ASML's shares were traded on the US stock exchange, the valuation of the company would likely be higher. In essence, European valuations of high-quality companies are generally lower compared to US counterparts.

I would want to own a business with a valuation of 12-month forward P/E of 31.3x and 20%+ annual EPS any day.

In my view, buying ASML's shares today is a low-hanging fruit with the potential for up to 17-18% total annual ROR over the next few years.

Investor's Takeaway

To be fair, the valuation of the US stock market makes me uneasy.

I am not one of the investors who, when shown a P/E ratio of 30x, immediately jumps ship, but I always view valuations in relation to growth expectations.

As it stands, buying a broader market simply doesn't justify the poor risk/reward, and it is more important than ever to be selective about asset allocation.

While I don't foresee a bubble, nor expect any bloodbath for US stocks, as the US continues to lead in GDP growth, I am careful with the companies I am buying, making sure I don't overpay.

Right now, I still see attractive value for investors in both Google and ASML.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Technology Has Come A Long Way, Baby, And It's Still Going Strong
data.description:
Back in the early 1980s when I first entered the corporate workforce, I was primarily working as a programmer/analyst, or what would now be called a software developer. I worked for several large Fortune 500 corporations, and we debated which would be the better personal computer, or PC, in the future. Would it be the Macintosh from Apple (AAPL), first introduced over 40 years ago, or the Windows PC from Microsoft (MSFT)? The debate raged on for years and then those two companies expanded and diverged and are still leaders in technology today. In fact, Apple and Microsoft are two of the top holdings in The Technology Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLK).

As the technology sector has matured, growth has slowed and XLK delivered a lower return than the overall S&P 500 index over the past year, returning just 16.6% compared to the 23.4% return from the broad-based SPY.

Some technology trends that everyone hears about on a near daily basis include AI and Quantum Computing (you can read about an ETF that invests in quantum computing stocks, here) that are primary growth drivers for technology, but there are other trends that are not as often discussed that are also propelling technology forward.

Depending on what happens with interest rates in 2025 (further rate reductions would be a catalyst) and the potential impacts of tariffs on the global technology trade (which could negatively impact the tech sector in the short-term), there is still good potential for continued growth in technology for the next five to ten years. Although it may not outperform the broader S&P 500, I still believe there is a case to be made for investing in XLK in 2025, and I therefore rate the ETF a Buy.

What Are the Top Technology Trends in 2025?

According to this report from Gartner, the top 10 technology trends for 2025 include some you might expect and others you may have not yet heard of.

Of course, AI (artificial intelligence) is at the top of the trends list. The recent news out of China regarding the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) chatbot app has resulted in heightened awareness of the cost, advancements in technology, and the potential threats from the emerging applications of AI. More work needs to be done on the AI Governance front as well as developing better security around disinformation to prevent fraudulent account takeovers, for example.

The Evolution of Agentic AI

The trend towards “Agentic AI” is the next evolution of business benefits expected to be realized from autonomous AI “agents” that can plan, assist, augment, or offload the work of humans or more traditional software applications.

Salesforce (CRM), a top holding in XLK, specifically calls out Agentic AI as an area of focus for their business.

Not to be outdone, Microsoft is touting the use of AI-powered agents in this inside track blog post.

It’s important to acknowledge that adopting AI in the enterprise is a journey. In Microsoft Digital, we’ve adopted a maturity model for AI deployment in the enterprise. Early phases focus on using Microsoft 365 Copilot, grounded in enterprise data, to enhance knowledge discovery and retrieval. Later phases enable employees to act on that knowledge and even fully automate business workflows.

What is Post-Quantum Cryptography?

If you are not already familiar with quantum computing, you may be interested in my review of one of the key players in that field of technology, Rigetti Computing (RGTI), whose stock I rated Hold after their Q3 2024 earnings report (the stock is up more than 600% since then). This was what I wrote about the company:

Rigetti is one of several companies that is focused on developing a quantum computing architecture. The basic unit of measurement in the development of quantum computing solutions is the Qubit. The specific method used to develop qubits for quantum computing is unique to each solution developer and in the case of Rigetti, they use a superconductor approach to qubit design.

Now that the quantum computing architecture is evolving to the point that practical applications are forthcoming in the near future, the next technology trend is to develop systems to defend against those new, advanced capabilities. From the NIST.GOV website, here is an explanation of what the need is for post-quantum cryptography:

Encryption algorithms protect confidential electronic information, from email messages to medical records and financial statements, from unauthorized viewers. For decades, these algorithms have proved strong enough to defend against attacks using conventional computers that attempt to defeat the encryption. However, a new type of device under development called a quantum computer could break these algorithms, rendering our electronic secrets vulnerable to discovery.

To counter this looming threat, we need encryption methods that can stave off cyberattacks by both the conventional computers we know today and the quantum computers of tomorrow. These new methods are called post-quantum encryption algorithms.

One of the bigger players in the technology field that is working on those types of algorithms is Accenture (ACN), another XLK top holding, as detailed in this news release regarding their new QuSecure offering.

Accenture has made a strategic investment, through Accenture Ventures, in QuSecure™ a leader in post-quantum cybersecurity. Together, Accenture and QuSecure offer comprehensive post-quantum crypto agility solutions to help government agencies and private sector businesses mitigate emerging quantum risks.

Human-Machine Synergies are Emerging

Another emerging technology trend that is still in the early stages of development entails “spatial computing” technology, creating immersive experiences in gaming, e-commerce, media, and other consumer-oriented industries. From Gartner’s report on spatial computing:

Spatial computing relies on multiple immersive technologies, including:

  • Augmented reality: AR overlays virtual elements onto the real world. In the context of spatial computing, the AR aligns and orchestrates virtual content with physical objects to create seamless interactive experiences.
  • Mixed reality: Mixed reality is the merging of real and virtual worlds, where physical and graphical objects appear to interact and integrate naturally. MR includes an underlying group of technologies encompassing a spectrum of immersive displays and interactive systems.
  • Metaverse: Metaverse interconnects digital spaces in which users can interact, socialize and create. Spatial computing ensures the accurate positioning of users and synchronizes their actions.

Embedded technologies are also involved, including eye tracking to monitor what users are looking at, speech recognition to enable voice commands, and handheld controllers and motion sensors that enable users to touch and move virtual objects. 5G and 6G technology is also required to provide the necessary speed and bandwidth for real-time interaction.

The technology is complex, requiring three layers of infrastructure, information, and interaction working together to provide a seamless user experience.

Broadcom (AVGO), another top holding in XLK, offers a broad range of technology solutions in support of the infrastructure layer that extends to the information layer with its recent acquisition of VMWare.

Broadcom Inc. is a global technology leader that designs, develops and supplies a broad range of semiconductor, enterprise software and security solutions. Broadcom’s category-leading product portfolio serves critical markets including cloud, data center, networking, broadband, wireless, storage, industrial and enterprise software. Our solutions include service provider and enterprise networking and storage, mobile device and broadband connectivity, mainframe, cybersecurity and private and hybrid cloud infrastructure.

The VMWare product suite also puts Broadcom in the running for other new frontiers of computing such as Edge Intelligence, Cloud Foundation Automation, and other enterprise, cloud, and AI big data management capabilities.

Another top holding in XLK is another long-time player in the technology field, International Business Machines Corporation (IBM). Both my father and my uncle (on my mother’s side) once worked for IBM, which has evolved into a software behemoth in addition to being a hardware provider. In fact, at the recent Investors Day event, IBM presented a framework for sustainable 5% growth due to a multitude of expanding opportunities.

Big Blue identified expanding opportunities in software, consulting, infrastructure, hybrid cloud, generative artificial intelligence and eventually quantum computing.

Oppenheimer recently upgraded IBM to outperform due to the software advancements being made by “Big Blue”.

IBM was initiated with an Outperform rating by Oppenheimer due in part to double-digit revenue growth in its software segment, driven by its Red Hat offerings.

IBM stock gained more than 42% in the past year after trading mostly sideways for the prior 10-year period.

Wall Street analysts give mostly Buy ratings to IBM stock today.

Oracle: Key Partner in the Stargate AI Project

Another long-term player in technology is software giant Oracle Corporation (ORCL). Initially founded in 1977 by Larry Ellison, Oracle is now a global leader in enterprise information technology environments. Oracle is set to be one of the companies to benefit from the recently announced Stargate AI Project, a planned $500B investment in technology over the next four years. Oracle plans to work closely with Nvidia and OpenAI on the project.

As part of Stargate, Oracle, NVIDIA, and OpenAI will closely collaborate to build and operate this computing system. This builds on a deep collaboration between OpenAI and NVIDIA going back to 2016 and a newer partnership between OpenAI and Oracle.

Microsoft will also benefit from the Stargate project as a result of its existing partnership with OpenAI.

This also builds on the existing OpenAI partnership with Microsoft. OpenAI will continue to increase its consumption of Azure as OpenAI continues its work with Microsoft with this additional compute to train leading models and deliver great products and services.

Nvidia Still a Strong Buy

Nvidia (NVDA) will also realize some potentially significant benefits from the Stargate project if it materializes as advertised. As the second-largest holding in XLK, the future of NVDA stock will have a big impact on the performance of XLK in the future. Despite delivering an 88% return over the past year, most Wall Street analysts still rate NVDA a Strong Buy.

The concerns regarding DeepSeek taking market share from Nvidia are overblown and may have resulted in an opportunity as investors bid down the price of NVDA stock after the news came out. At least one analyst suggests that NVDA will still outperform despite those concerns.

Nvidia was added to the Tactical Outperform list at Evercore ISI on Monday ahead of its fiscal fourth-quarter results, slated to be released on Feb. 26. "We are buyers of NVDA into its Jan-25Q earnings call scheduled for 26-Feb," analyst Mark Lipacis wrote in a note to clients. "NVDA has underperformed SPX by 9% over the past month, which we attribute to three investor concerns, namely that: DeepSeek lowers AI demand in aggregate; DeepSeek shifts AI compute-cycles away from NVDA GPUs and to ASICs; Blackwell delays."

After conducting a survey, Lipacis said that concerns over DeepSeek lowering aggregate demand are not true and that its lower cost is "evolutionary rather than revolutionary" and that lower cost and compute is likely to result in increased demand for tokens, which should result in larger parameter models and an acceleration of multi-modal tokens.

Nvidia is still the platform of choice for hyperscalers' customers, with it being 5 to 10 years ahead of "anything else in the market," Lipacis added.

Apple: Slowing Growth but Not Dead (Money) Yet

Currently, the largest holding in XLK at 14.5%, Apple Inc. has a market cap of over $3.5T, returned 32% over the past year, and gets mostly Strong Buy ratings from Wall Street analysts, although opinions differ more than they do on NVDA which has no Sell ratings.

When I last wrote about Apple in October 2022, I warned about their China Conundrum. The following bullet points were summarized from that article:

  • Apple has a long and complicated relationship with China, with the vast majority of products manufactured there.
  • New Chinese party leadership may cause problems for Apple and other US companies as Xi Jinping's power grows stronger.
  • Slowing demand in China from Covid lockdowns is offset by increasing demand for Macs, iPhone demand outside of China, and other innovations like the Apple Watch.

Apparently, Tim Cook and his crew were able to successfully navigate the changing political and cultural climate in China because the stock is up over 65% in total return since that article was published, slightly outperforming the S&P 500 over that same period.

In fact, just today, as I am doing research on this technology review, Apple announced that they plan to introduce new AI features in China by May of this year.

On Thursday, it was reported that Apple continues to work with Chinese company Baidu (BIDU) to develop AI features for iPhones in China even after bringing in another local partner Alibaba (BABA). Apple and Alibaba have submitted the AI features to China's cyberspace regulator for approval. In China, AI products require regulatory approval before they are launched for public use. In addition, Chinese regulations require Apple to collaborate with a local company to develop any AI applications for consumer use.

According to at least one Wall Street analyst, the China AI move represents a big step forward for Apple.

“While Apple has lost some share to Huawei over the past year, we believe the tide will start to turn over the coming year between the Apple Intelligence launch on iPhone 16 and then iPhone 17 launch in September,” Wedbush Securities analyst Dan Ives wrote in a note to clients.

Ives, who reiterated his Outperform rating and $325 price target on Apple, added that Apple Intelligence is a “catalyst around upgrades,” as Apple said the launch of Apple Intelligence helped boost iPhone upgrade rates in the markets it was available in. And now with China set to get it, perhaps by April or May, this is a “golden goose” scenario for Apple, Ives added.

Summary of XLK Characteristics Suggest Buy Rating

As of February 13, 2025, the XLK ETF had about $75B in assets under management, with 69 holdings that provide a representative selection of the technology sector index of the S&P 500. The gross expense ratio of the fund was reduced to 0.08% as of January 2025 (from 0.09% prior). The weighted average market cap of the fund holdings is about $1.5B. The fund offers a 0.64% distribution yield based on quarterly distributions.

The fund is a bit expensive based on the price/book ratio as well as the current average P/E as shown on the fund website. However, the estimated 3-5 year EPS growth* of 18% supports the Buy rating, in my opinion.

* Based on the underlying holdings of the fund. The actual earnings estimate for the underlying holdings are provided by FactSet Estimates and are used to calculate a mean 3-5 year EPS growth rate estimate.

Based on macroeconomic conditions and the recent inflows into risk-on sectors including technology as discussed in this insight from State Street, the conditions are favorable for continued outperformance by XLK.

Given the broad-based fervor for AI, technology ETFs had inflows once again in December. The $582 million of inflows increased tech ETFs’ lead versus all other sectors in 2024. In fact, their $33 billion represents 76% of all sector ETF flows in 2024 — well above their 37% market share of sector assets.

Given the recent favorable investor sentiment, healthy economic conditions, steady interest rates, and a positive continued growth outlook for the technology sector, especially in the top holdings as described above, I believe that XLK will deliver another strong performance in 2025.

data.title: DraftKings: Q4 Earnings, Bold 2025 Ambition But A Corresponding Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
DraftKings Inc. (NASDAQ:DKNG) has continued on the company’s path to a leading U.S. online gambling giant. The recent Q4 report highlights continued progress through strong user growth, despite the quarter’s financials lagging due to adverse NFL results. For 2025, DraftKings guides very ambitious growth, driven by several factors but potentially threatened by main competitor FanDuel’s market share gains against DraftKings. FanDuel is owned by Flutter Entertainment (FLUT).

In my previous August 2024 article on the stock, “DraftKings: Another Step Closer Into A Profitable Giant”, I initiated DraftKings at a Buy rating due to the company’s attractive revenue growth and margin expansion prospects, achieved through rapid industry growth and the company’s great competitive position. After the article was published, DraftKings’ stock has now returned 67% compared to the S&P 500’s return of 14% as DraftKings’ stock most recently rallied 15% to the better-than-expected outlook after Q4.

DraftKings’ Q4 Financials Came In As Expected

After much better revenue and earnings growth in previous quarters, DraftKings’ Q4 results came in nearly as expected at a weaker level - customer-friendly NFL season outcomes affected sportsbook hold percentages negatively across the industry for the quarter.

DraftKings' revenues grew 13.2% to $1393 million, well below the 30.1% growth for the full year, but in line with Wall Street’s already adjusted consensus estimate. Underlying handle in sports betting grew 15.1% year-on-year from a strong previous Q4, and iGaming revenues reached $426 million, up 21.0% year-on-year, while the adverse NFL results weakened revenues below previous expectations with a 0.4 percentage point decline in the net revenue margin.

As can be expected, the temporarily weak net revenue margin also led to weak profitability after DraftKings’ previously rapid margin expansion. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $89.5 million, down $61.6 million year-on-year. Underneath, DraftKings’ sales and marketing expenses also grew $77.8 million to $368.6 million after previously moderating marketing expenses throughout 2024, while other costs grew more moderately amidst the company’s expansion. The adjusted EPS of $0.14 still beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.10 despite the hiccup in sales and the increase in marketing expenses.

I don’t ultimately believe the higher marketing expenses to be an issue over the longer term – DraftKings grew its monthly users acceleratingly fast to 4.8 million in Q4 from 3.5 million a year ago, as the increased marketing spend seems to have paid off. DraftKings’ 2025 guidance also suggests profitability to expand rapidly, and marketing expenses don’t seem to be slowing down profitability improvements going forward.

Summing up, while DraftKings’ Q4 financials were weaker on the surface, I believe that the report still came with positive signals. The adverse NFL results are a transitory negative factor for the quarter alone, seen across the industry, with Flutter lowering its 2024 guidance due to the same reason. DraftKings showed very good user growth, setting the company up for a good 2025 as the company continues to scale.

The 2025 Guidance Expects Bold Growth

After the more moderate Q4 performance, DraftKings even slightly raised the company’s revenue guidance for 2025 with the Q4 report. The company now expects revenues of $6.3-6.6 billion for the year, coupled with reaffirmed adjusted EBITDA of $0.9-1.0 billion. At the mid-point, revenues are expected to grow 35.3%, and adjusted EBITDA is expected to grow $769 million through very rapid margin expansion – adjusted EBITDA came in at just $181 million for the entirety of 2024.

A good portion of the guided revenue growth seems to be anticipated to be coming from a better hold percentage. DraftKings had a 9.4% sportsbook hold percentage in 2024 against a structural hold percentage of 10.5%, but anticipates the hold percentage to increase to around 11% in 2025 through slightly increasing the structural hold and with actual hold converging with the structural hold after weak 2024 outcomes. The anticipated hold percentage increase would already imply around 17% revenue growth in sports betting even without handle growth, with DraftKings already pointing towards strong achieved hold percentages in early 2025.

Momentum in sports betting handle should also continue. As the Q4 report pointed out, DraftKings’ monthly users have continued to grow, contributing to improving handle. The company is continuing to expand its market presence as regulation allows, expecting to soon launch the sportsbook in Puerto Rico and Missouri while already established markets continue to mature. On top, DraftKings’ iGaming revenue has grown well while still covering only 5 states, contributing to good revenue growth expectations.

The raised revenue guidance is still quite ambitious, and could falter to competition from especially FanDuel – during Q4, FanDuel regained the number one spot in sports betting market share from DraftKings, and the two market leaders now already have quite a large gap in market share as of December. Casino Reports estimates DraftKings’ 34.8% market share in sports betting to have a 3.2 percentage point gap to FanDuel’s. FanDuel has also gained some ground against DraftKings’ market share in iGaming during Q4, but DraftKings has regardless continued to have quite a stable market share in iGaming while the market share loss in sports betting has been more pronounced. I also wrote on the two companies’ market share rivalry in my January article on Flutter.

Importantly, the growth is finally expected to scale profitability to a meaningfully positive level. At the guidance mid-point, the adjusted EBITDA margin is expected at 14.0%, still way below Flutter’s and many other operators’ margin level in mature markets, but already expected to generate $850 million in free cash flow.

DraftKings’ Earnings Potential Is Now Likely Priced In

I slightly updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for DraftKings’ stock.

As DraftKings’ user growth has been very good and the company guides for a good 2025, I slightly raised my revenue expectations. I expect the $6.45 billion revenue mid-point for 2025, after which I now estimate a total revenue CAGR of 9.0% up until 2034. After the year, I estimate slightly elevated perpetual growth of 3%. As previously, I estimate DraftKings’ profitability to gradually improve and reach a 29.2% adjusted EBITDA margin. DraftKings itself targets the margin to reach a level close to 30%.

I again estimate a 10% dilution to DraftKings’ outstanding shares to offset stock-based compensation, and slightly raised my cash flow conversion expectations to a good level due to a strong 2025 free cash flow guidance.

The estimates put DraftKings’s fair value estimate at $48.67, 9% below the stock price at the time of writing. Although DraftKings has a very good growth outlook ahead, I now believe that the stock’s risk-to-reward has become more balanced from the previously attractive level as the stock has risen considerably after my previous article. A much higher fair value would likely need aggressive and successful market expansion beyond North America.

The fair value estimate has stayed nearly stagnant from $48.64 previously, as moderately improved cash flow estimates are offset by a higher WACC.

CAPM

Compared to 9.68% previously, the DCF model now uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.42%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

While DraftKings has some debt, I don’t estimate the company to use a notable amount of leverage over the long term. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.48% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.98. With an ESG addon of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 10.48%.

Takeaway

DraftKings’ Q4 financials were expectedly weak, as NFL results affected the industry’s hold percentage very negatively. Underneath, DraftKings rapidly gained new users with increased marketing spend, and the company even slightly raised its 2025 revenue guidance to $6.3-6.6 million. The current year is expected to show a much better hold percentage after weakness in both 2023 and 2024, and continued market expansion and user growth should continue to expand sports betting handle and iGaming revenues. Importantly, the expected growth is now guided to also contribute to rapid EBITDA growth.

Despite the positive growth outlook, I downgrade my rating on DraftKings to Hold. The valuation now prices in DraftKings’ earnings potential after the rally, and FanDuel’s recent market share gains against DraftKings pose a slight growth risk against very strong growth expectations from the market.

data.title: AMD: The AI Contender With A Significant Correction, But It Isn't Cheap Yet
data.description:
Overview

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) (TSX:AMD:CA) is one of the most prominent semiconductor companies in the world. It has been the main competitor of Intel (INTC) in the desktop and laptop market; it has been the leading provider of microchips in the gaming consoles Xbox from Microsoft and PSP from Sony. Lately, it is the only company competing with Nvidia (NVDA) in the hot GPU for AI market. As you can see in Figure 1, the stock price has decreased by 35% in the last 12 months.

AMD has always been a technological follower and a lower-price competitor. This has happened in the desktop and laptop market chasing Intel, which is happening right now with Nvidia and the Data Center GPU market. The company is going to benefit from AI euphoria and take advantage of the new wave of AI PCs, but it is exposed to relevant risks. However, technological competition in those markets is intensifying and is stressing margins.

Like most competitors in the industry, the company is getting high valuation multiples that I consider out of reach in relation to its business fundamentals.

A clear Data Center opportunity

The two most lucrative markets for AMD are the Data Center and the Client segment. Both markets have their challenges, but in general, they are compelling big business opportunities. The Data Center segment is dominated by NVIDIA, and here, the risk is to invest high but be an insignificant player. This segment grew 94% year over year to $12.6 billion in 2024, driven by EPYC CPUs and Instint GPUs. However, Nvidia remains the dominant player in AI GPUs, with an 80% market share. Nvidia’s dominance is related to its leadership in GPU technology and CUDA software, as the company is trying to create an AI platform with network effects.

Nvidia has opened a very lucrative new market, and for me, it is not a competitor. I see it as a facilitator that is opening a vast opportunity for AMD: I think AMD will gain market share in this expansive market as a low-cost producer, especially when requirements are not so demanding as to necessitate Nvidia’s GPU. Sometimes, the opportunity will come from a lack of availability in Nvidia’s inventory.

The only companies that can overshadow AMD’s performance are those that personalize chips for hyperscalers: Broadcom (AVGO) or Marvell Technology (MRVL). I don’t think it will hurt much of AMD’s business. Broadcom GPU business in Q4 was $300 million, and I estimate AMD business in the last quarter is $800 million. Personalized business doesn’t scale and also lacks a common software platform, like ROCm.

Anyway, growth rates will be high, around 40%, a little bit lower than management has stated in the mid-term. This segment has the highest margin, is double the Client and Gaming segment, and is growing the fastest (93.6% vs 51.7% in the Client segment). However, this explosive growth won’t last forever; I estimate that the high AI growth will last four or five more years. Later, it will moderate its growth.

Taking advantage of Intel’s weakness

The other segment, Client, is dominated by desktops and laptops, and the main competitor has been Intel, see Figure 2. However, Intel is facing some strategic challenges. Once an integrated semiconductor player dominates the design and manufacturing of the CPU market with the x86 architecture, the manufacturing capabilities are now a drag on the design, and it is unable to adapt to market requirements. AMD is a design company that is gaining market share without the constraints of manufacturing facilities.

Intel's weakness is allowing AMD to increase prices, positioning it as an innovation leader in the market. The price of mobile and desktop Ryzen processors will increase by 14% in 2024.

However, AMD is facing competition from other players like Qualcomm (QCOM), which supports ARM’s architecture, which is the design win for energy-efficient and smaller devices. Those characteristics are critical for new AI devices.

Margins are hard to keep in disruptive times

Margins in the microprocessor industry are healthy, but we can observe some deterioration in practically all the players except Nvidia, which we could consider a special case (Figure 3). The table uses Cash Margin, which involves adjusting net income for non-cash items such as amortization and depreciation, stock-based compensation, and deferred income tax.

The margin deterioration is due to the intensity of product competition around AI, which causes companies to invest more in research and development. AMD has increased R&D expenses from 17-21% of revenue in 2021 and 2022 to 25-26% in the last two years. The company launched the MI325 AI accelerator last quarter. MI350 series is announced to be launched in the middle of the year, and the company keeps developing its developers’ software platform ROCm, trying to catch up with Nvidia’s CUDA platform.

I foresee that margins will continue at that level during times of technological disruption and will improve later in more stable times. Data Center margins are higher, and I think AMD will keep gaining market share in the desktop and laptop market over Intel. It will give more scale, and as we have already seen, it will raise its prices. AMD will be the second-best player in each of the Data Center and Client markets, positioning the company in the 20% range of cash margin.

Valuation

Figure 4 shows the company's value drivers, considering a year as the last four quarters to capture the latest information.

My revenue estimation is based on high growth in the Data Center segment, but not as fast as last year. It will be 40% until 2029, and then it will decrease to 25%. The Client segment will grow at a healthy 25% in 2025, 20% until 2031, and then 10%. The Gaming and Embedded segments will keep decreasing in 2025 and 2026 due to the inventory’s normalization, and later, they will grow slightly ahead of inflation, which is quite conservative. I estimate that revenue growth could grow five percentage points higher than my base case and eight percentage points lower, and I have modeled this risk as a normal distribution.

The cash margin will remain stable until 2027, a period of high AI innovation and investment in research and development. Later, it will grow slightly to 20% and 21% in 2032-2034. The improvement is due to lower research and development investment and a better position in the Client segment thanks to a market share gain over Intel, as we have discussed previously. Those cash margins can be up three percentage points over the base case or two percentage points under the base case, and I have also modeled it as a normal distribution.

Net working capital variation and CAPEX will remain at 6% of revenue and 2% of revenue, respectively. Cash flows will be discounted at an 11.3% WACC because the beta is 1.71. The risk-free rate is 4.5%. The company's leverage is insignificant. The perpetual growth rate is set at 3%.

Based on Figure 5, I estimate a value of $123 per share, a 10% premium over its stock price. As I have declared in my previous articles, this premium doesn’t have a margin of safety enough to be a healthy investment. When I run the normal distribution of revenue growth and cash margin, the risk software Crystal Ball tells us that there is a 54% chance of getting a value higher than the current stock price. The revenue growth rate explains 86% of the variation of the value and the cash margin explains 14%.

In Figure 6, you can see that the multiple is the highest of the companies that we have talked about. The company has suffered a correction from the beginning of last year, and the current multiple is more reasonable. It is even higher than Nvidia’s (x59) and Broadcom’s (x57), which are two of the hottest stocks in the market. In the third tier, Qualcomm and Intel are facing structural problems, as we have mentioned.

The main risk is the ARM technology

AMD's main strategic priority is to use and promote the x86 architecture. It is collaborating with Intel through the x86 Ecosystem Advisory Group to strengthen the architecture and enhance compatibility across x86 platforms. This collaboration includes players like Broadcom, Dell, Google, HPE, and Microsoft.

Unlike Intel, AMD doesn’t manufacture its chips, and this fabless strategy allows it to be more flexible in adopting new architectures. This flexibility is what AMD can use in ARM technology. AMD holds a license and has stated its willingness to produce ARM chips but is not currently using them. The company has integrated ARM TrustZone security technology into its processors. They even developed ARM cores, but they discontinued these developments. I think AMD is not interested in ARM technology because, with x86 architecture, there is just one competitor, Intel, whose financial health is weak. AMD is gaining market share due to this weakness on Intel. Promoting ARM technology will increase the number of competitors like Qualcomm. This technological trend towards ARM is happening. For example, Qualcomm is gaining a 10% market share in PCs above $800.

My hypothesis is that the evolution will tend to reduce x86 architecture chips favoring ARM’s but AMD will have the flexibility to capture some of the market with ARM technology later on.

Recently, Meta has announced the use of ARM technology in its data center servers, displacing the chips that used to be provided by Intel and AMD.

Conclusion

AMD’s stock price decreased 35% last year, but I estimate its value is still in line with the current stock price. For a buying opportunity, the stock price has to be reduced further. I recommend holding on to the stock because AMD is a clear winner in the AI space, with flexible capabilities to adapt and take advantage of AI expansion.

data.title: TSM Can Be A Surprise Winner In The Tariff War
data.description:
TSMC (NYSE:TSM) (OTC:TSMWF) and the entire chip industry have been shaken by the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) announcement. We have also seen announcements of new AI models by Alibaba (BABA) and ByteDance. It is highly likely that we will continue to see more efficient large language models, LLMs, and more efficient AI tools. This will inevitably lead to some demand adjustment in the short term. However, TSM is the market leader and the main provider of chip manufacturing to most of the tech industry. After the dismissal of the former Intel (INTC) CEO, it is possible that Intel’s foundry effort will take a back seat. This improves the moat for TSM and should allow the company to capture future demand. In the previous article, it was mentioned that TSM could give greater certainty within this industry.

TSM is already looking to run Intel's factories within the US after a request by the White House. Despite the trade tensions, TSM has a strong lead which can improve its position in this industry. TSM beat the recent earnings estimate, and it is expected that TSM will deliver 28% YoY EPS growth in the fiscal year ending Dec 2025. This is a massive growth rate, and it takes the forward PE multiple for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 to less than 20. TSM is showing strong growth while having a modest multiple, making it very attractive to investors looking to ride out the short-term fluctuations.

Tailwinds Amid Tariff Tensions

Another major tailwind for TSM is the current uncertainty in Intel. The former Intel CEO made a massive investment in order to ramp up the foundry business of the company. Intel received subsidies from the domestic United States government and many other governments. TSM is already looking to increase investment in the US and is looking to run Intel's factories after a request by the White House. Intel is also showing very low yields in its ambitious 18A node. TSM has a big lead over Intel in the process node and this is unlikely to change in the near to medium term.

TSM is a clear winner from these changes, and it could build an unassailable lead over the next few years. Lower competitive challenges should be a positive for TSM’s ability to deliver better revenue and margin numbers. Any investment in new foundry requires massive investments and there are very few companies who would want to enter this segment after the results shown by Intel. This gives TSM a very good moat over the long term.

DeepSeek announcement changes the game

The focus of most of the tech industry in the last two years has been to buy as much computing power as possible in order to build better AI models. However, DeepSeek-R1 model has announced a 95% cost reduction over the earlier OpenAI model. Some of the hyperscalers have already deployed DeepSeek in order to give their clients the benefit of these efficiencies. Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS deployed DeepSeek a few days back and is already seeing improvements due to it. Similarly, Microsoft (MSFT) has also deployed this model on its cloud platform.

Figure: Amazon’s AWS deploying DeepSeek. Source: AWS

Other companies including Alibaba and ByteDance have also announced launching better models. It is highly likely that we will see a more prudent investment strategy on AI platforms which also tries to improve efficiency. Meta (META) and other big tech players have not announced any reduction in their capex plans yet.

TSM provides chips to almost all these tech companies, and we could see some short-term headwinds for the revenue growth. However, TSM has a massive moat and any demand postponed will eventually be captured by the company.

Good earnings and forward estimates

TSM recently announced strong earnings which beat the EPS and revenue estimates. The forward estimates of EPS growth are also quite high. It is estimated that TSM will deliver 28% EPS growth in the current fiscal year ending Dec 2025. It will deliver another 18% growth in the fiscal year ending Dec 2026. Despite the high forward growth estimate, TSM stock is trading at only 19.6 times the PE estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026.

Figure: Forward estimates of TSM. Source: Seeking Alpha

It should be noted that TSM stock had already reached close to $150 in early 2022 before the AI hype started. On the other hand, in early 2022 Nvidia (NVDA) was close to $30 or a quarter of the current price after adjusting for a stock split. Nvidia has reached the current level on the back of its AI chips alone, and we should see some correction in the stock. However, TSM has a more diverse revenue stream, and it will not be significantly affected by short-term fears about demand reduction.

Impact on stock trajectory

TSM is quite cheap when we look at its market leadership position, forward growth estimates, and its valuation multiple. When we look at EPS for 2 fiscal years ahead, Nvidia is trading at 22 times while TSM is trading at close to 19. Nvidia has seen its earnings improve massively in the past two years due to the AI investments. Any dip in these investments will have a massive impact on Nvidia. On the other hand, TSM’s earnings have grown at a more steady pace in the last few years, which reduces the headwinds on the stock due to any big changes in the AI industry.

Figure: Comparison of forward EPS of Nvidia and TSM
